title: Sensors and Perception Fundamentals
objective: Understand the major sensor types used in robotics, how they work, their strengths and limitations, and how sensor fusion combines multiple sources into a reliable perception of the world.
beginnerExplanation:
Imagine trying to walk through your house blindfolded, with earplugs in and wearing thick gloves. You would bump into furniture, trip over stairs, and have no idea where you were. That is what a robot without sensors is like — powerful motors and a fast computer, but completely disconnected from reality.

Robot sensors fall into two broad categories. Proprioceptive sensors measure the robot's own internal state — joint angles (encoders), acceleration (accelerometers), rotation rate (gyroscopes), and motor current. These tell the robot about itself. Exteroceptive sensors measure the external environment — distance to objects (LiDAR, ultrasonic), visual appearance (cameras), temperature, force, and more. These tell the robot about the world.

The most common range sensors in robotics are ultrasonic sensors (cheap, short range, low resolution — used on car parking sensors), infrared distance sensors (moderate range, moderate accuracy), and LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging — expensive, long range, very high accuracy, producing detailed 3D point clouds of the environment). Cameras are the richest single sensor, capturing millions of pixels of colour information, but converting that raw image data into useful information about objects and distances requires sophisticated computer vision algorithms.

No single sensor is perfect. LiDAR works in the dark but fails in heavy rain. Cameras provide rich detail but struggle in low light. GPS gives global position but not indoors. This is why real robots use sensor fusion — combining data from multiple complementary sensors to build a more complete and reliable picture of the world than any single sensor could provide alone.
deeperExplanation:
Sensor selection is one of the most critical design decisions in any robotic system. Each sensor type has a characteristic set of properties: measurement range, resolution, accuracy, update rate, field of view, sensitivity to environmental conditions, cost, size, weight, and power consumption. The roboticist must understand these properties to choose the right combination for their application.

Encoders are perhaps the most fundamental robot sensor. A rotary encoder converts the angular position of a shaft into a digital signal. Incremental encoders produce pulses as the shaft rotates — counting pulses gives relative position. Absolute encoders output a unique code for each angular position, providing absolute position even after power loss. Encoders on every joint of a robot arm are what allow the controller to know the arm's configuration at all times.

Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) combine accelerometers and gyroscopes (and sometimes magnetometers) to measure linear acceleration, angular velocity, and heading. They are essential for mobile robots and drones, providing high-frequency attitude estimation. However, IMUs suffer from drift — small measurement errors accumulate over time, causing the estimated position to diverge from reality. This is why IMUs are almost always fused with other sensors like GPS or visual odometry.

LiDAR sensors emit laser pulses and measure the time of flight to determine distance. Mechanical spinning LiDARs (like the Velodyne VLP-16) rotate a laser/detector assembly to scan the environment, producing hundreds of thousands of 3D points per second. Solid-state LiDARs are emerging as cheaper, more reliable alternatives. In Australian mining, LiDAR is the primary sensor for autonomous haul truck navigation, providing reliable range measurements in dust and varying light conditions.

Sensor fusion is the mathematical framework for combining measurements from multiple sensors. The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is the workhorse algorithm — it maintains a probabilistic estimate of the robot's state (position, velocity, orientation) and updates this estimate as new sensor measurements arrive, weighting each measurement by its expected accuracy. More advanced approaches include particle filters (for non-Gaussian distributions) and factor graph optimisation (used in modern SLAM systems). The key insight is that sensor fusion is not just averaging — it is a principled statistical framework that accounts for the uncertainty in each sensor and produces an estimate that is more accurate than any individual sensor.
keyTakeaways: Proprioceptive sensors measure internal state; exteroceptive sensors measure the environment
No single sensor is perfect — each has characteristic strengths and failure modes
LiDAR provides accurate 3D range data; cameras provide rich visual detail; IMUs provide high-frequency motion estimation
Sensor fusion combines multiple sensors into a more reliable estimate than any single source
The Extended Kalman Filter is the foundational algorithm for sensor fusion in robotics
example: An autonomous agricultural robot spraying weeds in a Queensland cotton field uses a GPS receiver for coarse global positioning (accurate to about 2 cm with RTK correction), a LiDAR scanner to detect crop rows and measure ground clearance, stereo cameras with computer vision to identify weeds versus cotton plants in real time, wheel encoders to track distance travelled, and an IMU to measure vehicle tilt on uneven terrain. The sensor fusion system combines all these inputs, using GPS and LiDAR for navigation between rows, cameras for real-time weed detection, and the IMU to compensate for vehicle roll and pitch that would otherwise cause the spray nozzles to miss their targets. If GPS signal is temporarily lost under tree canopy at the field edge, the system continues navigating using LiDAR and wheel odometry until GPS returns.
commonMistakes: Relying on a single sensor type — real-world systems need redundancy and complementary sensing
Ignoring sensor noise and assuming measurements are perfectly accurate
Forgetting that sensors have failure modes — LiDAR in heavy rain, cameras in direct sun, GPS indoors
Treating sensor fusion as simple averaging rather than a probabilistic estimation framework
Over-specifying expensive sensors when cheaper alternatives would suffice for the application
exercise: Set up a simple experiment using a smartphone. Use a compass app to measure heading, the accelerometer to detect tilt, and the camera to estimate distance to a nearby object. Record ten measurements of each. Calculate the mean and standard deviation. Which sensor is most consistent? Which drifts most? Write a short report explaining what you learned about sensor noise and why sensor fusion would help combine these three imperfect measurements into something more reliable.

quiz.question: What is the difference between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors?
options: Proprioceptive sensors are more expensive than exteroceptive sensors
Proprioceptive sensors measure the robot's internal state while exteroceptive sensors measure the external environment
Proprioceptive sensors are digital while exteroceptive sensors are analogue
Proprioceptive sensors are used in mobile robots while exteroceptive sensors are used in robot arms
quiz.explanation: Proprioceptive sensors (like encoders, IMUs, and current sensors) measure the robot's own state — joint angles, acceleration, motor load. Exteroceptive sensors (like cameras, LiDAR, and ultrasonic sensors) measure properties of the external environment — distance to objects, visual appearance, temperature. A well-designed robot uses both types.

quiz.question: Why is sensor fusion important in robotics?
options: It makes sensors cheaper by sharing components
It eliminates all sensor noise completely
It combines complementary sensors to produce a more reliable estimate than any single sensor alone
It allows robots to function with only one sensor type
quiz.explanation: Sensor fusion uses mathematical frameworks like the Kalman filter to combine measurements from multiple sensors, weighting each by its expected accuracy. Because different sensors have different strengths and failure modes, fusing them produces a more robust and accurate estimate of the robot's state and environment than relying on any single sensor.

perspectives.engineer: As a sensor engineer, you select, calibrate, and integrate sensor hardware. You understand the physics of each sensor type, design mounting and wiring, and write driver software that delivers clean, timestamped data to the perception stack.
perspectives.algorithmDeveloper: As a perception algorithm developer, you build the software that transforms raw sensor data into useful information — detecting objects, estimating distances, classifying terrain. You work with point clouds, images, and IMU streams.
perspectives.systemsIntegrator: As a systems integrator, you ensure all sensors work together harmoniously — synchronised in time, aligned in space, and fused through a common state estimation framework. You debug the failures that only appear when everything runs together.
